title: Infrared heating panels: running cost and are they worth it?
description: What infrared heating panels cost to run in the UK at 2026 prices, how they compare with gas and heat pumps, and where they actually earn their keep.
blurb: Hourly and winter running costs for infrared panels at 2026 rates, plus where they beat other heating and where they fall flat.
lede: Infrared heating panels are among the most heavily marketed heating products in the UK, sold on slim looks, silent running and bold claims about savings. The physics behind them is real enough: instead of warming the air, they radiate heat directly at people, walls and furniture, much like low winter sun warming your face. What the adverts tend to gloss over is that they run on full-price electricity, which costs more than three times as much per unit of heat as mains gas. This guide works out what panels actually cost to run at current prices, where they genuinely save money, and where they would be an expensive mistake.
short_answer: A typical 600W infrared panel costs about 15.7p per hour flat out, or roughly 9 to 10p per hour once a thermostat starts cycling it, at the July to September 2026 price cap rate of 26.11p per kWh. Run for five evening hours a day, that is around £14 over a winter month. Heating one occupied room this way instead of firing up the whole house can be money well spent. As a complete replacement for gas central heating, though, infrared panels would add roughly £900 to £1,600 a year to a typical bill.

sections.h2: How infrared panels work, and the truth about "100% efficient"
sections.html:
A conventional heater warms the air, and the air then warms you. An infrared panel skips the middleman: it emits far-infrared radiation that travels in straight lines and turns into heat when it lands on a solid surface, whether that is your skin, the sofa or the wall opposite. Panels are flat slabs, usually 300W to 1,200W, mounted on a wall or ceiling, with no moving parts, no maintenance and nothing to service. A basic 600W panel costs around £100 to £250, with glass and mirror finishes running to £300 or more, and a plug-in model is a five-minute DIY job.

Now the marketing claim. Sellers love to say infrared panels are "100% efficient". That is true, but it is equally true of a £15 fan heater, an oil-filled radiator and your electric kettle. Every electric resistance heater converts every kWh of electricity into exactly one kWh of heat. The efficiency figure tells you nothing about which heater is cheaper to run, because they all cost the same per unit of heat at the same electricity rate.

The genuine differences are about comfort and targeting:

  • Radiant heat lands on you directly, so many people feel comfortable at an air temperature 1 to 2 degrees lower than they would need with a convector. A lower setpoint means less energy.
  • Panels heat surfaces, not air, so less of your spend escapes through draughts or rises uselessly to the ceiling.
  • The flip side is line of sight. Sit behind the sofa, around a corner or under a desk and the panel does very little for you until the room surfaces slowly catch up.

sections.h2: What an infrared panel costs to run
sections.html:
Running cost is simple to work out: wattage divided by 1,000, times hours, times your unit rate. A panel left switched on runs at full power continuously. Paired with a thermostat, it cycles on and off once the room reaches temperature, and in a reasonably insulated room it will typically be drawing power around 60% of the time.

Panel sizePer hour, full powerPer hour, thermostat cycling5 hours a day, per month
350W9.1p5.5p£8.20
600W15.7p9.4p£14.10
900W23.5p14.1p£21.15
1,200W31.3p18.8p£28.20

At 26.11p per kWh, Ofgem price cap July to September 2026. The thermostat columns assume the panel draws power about 60% of the time once the room is warm; the monthly figure uses the thermostat rate over 30 days.

For sizing, the usual rule of thumb is 50 to 60 watts per square metre of floor area in a well-insulated room, rising to 80 to 100 watts per square metre in older, draughtier homes. A typical 12 square metre home office therefore wants a 600W to 900W panel. Undersizing is a false economy: a panel that is too small runs flat out all day and never gets the room comfortable.

sections.h2: Infrared vs gas, heat pumps and other electric heaters
sections.html:
This is where the honest comparison has to happen, because the fuel matters far more than the heater.

  • Mains gas: at 7.33p per kWh through a modern condensing boiler running at roughly 90% efficiency, a kWh of useful heat costs about 8.1p. The same kWh from an infrared panel costs 26.11p, more than three times as much.
  • Heat pumps: an air source heat pump turning each kWh of electricity into 3 or more kWh of heat delivers warmth at roughly 7.5 to 8.7p per kWh, on a par with gas and roughly a third of the cost of infrared.
  • Other electric heaters: fan heaters, oil-filled radiators, convectors and infrared panels all pay the same 26.11p per kWh. Infrared can sometimes do the same comfort job with a smaller wattage or a lower setpoint, especially in tall or draughty rooms, but the saving is in how much heat you need, never in the price of the heat itself. Our guide to electric heater types compared goes through the trade-offs in detail.

Scale that up and the whole-house arithmetic gets brutal. A typical UK home burns somewhere around 9,000 to 10,000 kWh of gas a year for space heating, costing roughly £700 to £750 in gas units. Delivering similar comfort with infrared panels, even granting a generous 30% reduction from zoning and lower setpoints, would cost around £1,650 a year in electricity. With no reduction it is closer to £2,300. That is an extra £900 to £1,600 every year, which no amount of saved boiler servicing will claw back.

sections.h2: Where infrared panels genuinely earn their keep
sections.html:
None of that makes infrared panels a bad product. It makes them a targeting tool rather than a whole-house heating system, and used that way they can save real money.

  • Heating one occupied room: a 600W panel warming your home office for a working day costs less than £1, against several pounds for running gas central heating through the whole house. The maths in whether it is cheaper to heat one room or the whole house applies directly, and infrared is one of the best single-room tools available.
  • Bathrooms: mirror panels double as a demisting mirror, warm you the instant they switch on, and have no moving parts or exposed elements. A short burst of comfort on demand is exactly what radiant heat is good at.
  • Conservatories, garages and workshops: spaces that leak air faster than you can warm it suit radiant heat, because the panel heats you and the workbench rather than the air rushing out through the gaps.
  • Homes off the gas grid: against old storage heaters or plug-in convectors, panels compete well on control and comfort, although a heat pump remains far cheaper to run if the budget and building allow one.
  • Cold, damp wall spots: a panel aimed at a chronically cold external wall raises the surface temperature, which cuts the condensation that feeds mould. It is a running-cost commitment, but cheaper than redecorating every spring.
  • Rentals and awkward installs: no flue, no plumbing, no annual service. For a landlord adding heat to a converted loft room or a granny annexe, a hardwired panel with a thermostat is about the simplest compliant option there is.

If you do run infrared panels, a few habits and pairings make a large difference to what they cost.

  • Never run one without a thermostat. A bare panel on a switch runs at full power until you remember it. A built-in or plug-in thermostat, £15 to £30 for the plug-in kind, typically cuts consumption by a third or more once the room is up to temperature.
  • Draughtproof and insulate first. Radiant heat copes with draughts better than warm air does, but a leaky room still needs a bigger panel running longer. Cheap sealing work shrinks the wattage you need to buy and to feed.
  • Mount it where it can see you. Ceiling mounting above the desk or sofa beats a wall position behind furniture. Line of sight is the whole point of the technology.
  • Pair with solar carefully. The catch is seasonal: solar panels generate the least in December and January, exactly when heating demand peaks, so free midwinter heating is not realistic. Infrared pairs more usefully with solar in the chilly, bright shoulder months of spring and autumn, and the wider sums in whether solar is worth it stand on their own merits.
  • Consider a battery and a cheap overnight window. Because infrared gives instant heat rather than stored heat, off-peak tariffs do little for it directly. A home battery changes that: charge it overnight at an off-peak rate and your panels effectively run on cheap electricity all evening. See solar battery storage for how the storage side works and what it costs.

Infrared panels are honest, simple heaters wrapped in overexcited marketing. They convert electricity to heat at exactly the same rate as every other electric heater, and at 26.11p per kWh that heat costs over three times what mains gas delivers. As a replacement for gas central heating they would add somewhere between £900 and £1,600 a year to a typical home's bills, and a heat pump beats them on running cost by a factor of three.

Used with precision, the verdict flips. A 600W panel with a thermostat heating the one room you actually occupy costs around 9 to 10p an hour, or £14 a month for five hours a day. For home offices, bathrooms, conservatories, off-gas rooms and chronic cold spots, that is a fair price for instant, silent, zero-maintenance warmth, and often cheaper than heating the whole house for the sake of one person. Buy them as a scalpel, not a furnace.

faq.q: How much does it cost to run a 600W infrared panel for an hour?
faq.a: About 15.7p per hour at full power, or roughly 9 to 10p per hour once a thermostat is cycling it in a warm room, at the Ofgem price cap rate of 26.11p per kWh for July to September 2026.

faq.q: Are infrared panels cheaper to run than oil-filled radiators?
faq.a: Not per unit of heat. Both convert every kWh of electricity into one kWh of heat at the same price. Infrared can work out cheaper in practice if its radiant warmth lets you use a smaller wattage or a lower thermostat setting for the same comfort, particularly in draughty or high-ceilinged rooms.

faq.q: Can infrared panels replace gas central heating?
faq.a: Technically yes, financially almost never. Heating a typical house entirely with infrared would cost roughly £900 to £1,600 a year more than gas at current rates. The exceptions are small, very well insulated homes with no gas connection, where the low purchase and installation cost can outweigh the higher unit rate.

faq.q: Do infrared heating panels work well with solar panels?
faq.a: Only partly. Solar output is at its lowest in midwinter, exactly when heating demand peaks, so panels will not give you free heating in January. They pair better with solar in spring and autumn, or with a home battery charged on a cheap overnight tariff that then runs the panels in the evening.

faq.q: What size infrared panel do I need for my room?
faq.a: Allow roughly 50 to 60 watts per square metre of floor area in a well-insulated room, or 80 to 100 watts per square metre in an older, draughtier home. A typical 12 square metre office needs a 600W to 900W panel. Go a size up if in doubt, since an undersized panel runs constantly without ever making the room comfortable.
